Original Persona: A biomedical engineer working on developing innovative respiratory devices in partnership with the pulmonologist
Expanded Persona:
Dr. Elena Reyes (she/her) is a Mixed race biomedical engineer born in 1941 who has spent her career developing innovative respiratory devices in partnership with pulmonologists. With more than five decades of experience, she combines hands-on design, clinical trials oversight, and regulatory expertise. Now an emeritus engineer and consultant, she mentors interdisciplinary teams and advises on real-world device integration. Her work emphasizes patient-centered, durable, and accessible respiratory technologies informed by direct clinical collaboration. She also remains active publishing, teaching part-time, and advocating for equitable access to respiratory care.

09/09/1959.Event: Begins an undergraduate engineering program focused on applied mechanics and electronics; commits to a multi-year course of study that shapes a long-term preference for hands-on design and technical rigor.

10/05/1959.Event: Today spends the afternoon in the campus workshop sketching respiratory device prototypes and soldering small circuits, demonstrating a daily preference for practical, hands-on work.

11/12/1960.Event: Today keeps a careful lab notebook, eats quick simple lunches between experiments, and maintains a routine of documenting observations — a short-term habit that supports longer research commitments.

12/11/1961.Event: Today encounters a field report of an early prototype failing under real-world use; this incident reinforces a strong dislike of poorly durable devices and motivates a focus on robustness.

06/15/1962.Event: Joins a biomedical research lab working on respiratory support devices, beginning a sustained multi-year role collaborating with clinicians and refining device concepts in clinical contexts.

07/01/1963.Event: Today attends a weekly interdisciplinary meeting with clinicians and physiologists; values these exchanges but notes frustration when teams work in silos, reflecting a short-term reaction to collaboration dynamics.

09/01/1964.Event: Starts a formal, ongoing collaboration with pulmonologists to co-design patient-centered respiratory devices, establishing a long-term practice of clinical partnership and iterative feedback.

02/14/1965.Event: Today voices frustration about regulatory paperwork and slow approval processes that delay device deployment, illustrating a short-term annoyance that repeatedly influences project timelines.

05/20/1966.Event: Leads her first multi-site clinical trial oversight; the trial highlights a design element that does not align with patient use patterns, creating a long-term commitment to prioritize patient-centered design and oppose solutions that ignore end-user needs.

03/10/1968.Event: Begins sustained advocacy efforts for equitable access to respiratory care while also taking on regular part-time teaching and early publishing; these decisions mark long-term priorities in mentorship, dissemination, and social impact.

03/11/1968.Event: Today deliberately avoids the workbench and delegates soldering and prototyping tasks to a hands-on apprentice after noticing increasing weakness and fine-motor tremor in her dominant hand.
